key_points: Elections are the process through which citizens choose their representatives in a democracy
Democratic elections must offer real choices to voters among different political parties and candidates
Political competition takes place among parties and candidates to win elections
In India every citizen aged 18 years and above has the right to vote
This system of giving voting rights to all adults is called Universal Adult Franchise
The country is divided into different constituencies for the purpose of elections
A voters list or electoral roll contains the names of all eligible voters in a constituency
Some constituencies are reserved for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes
Election campaigns are conducted by political parties to attract voters
The Election Commission of India conducts and supervises elections
examples: Example of national election: Lok Sabha election
Example of state election: Legislative Assembly election
Example of election technology: Electronic Voting Machine (EVM)
Example of reserved constituency: Seats reserved for SC/ST candidates
Example of election activity: Political rallies and speeches
Example of voter participation: Citizens casting votes at polling booths
formulas: Election → Process of choosing representatives through voting
Universal Adult Franchise → Right of all citizens aged 18 or above to vote
Constituency → A geographical area that elects one representative
Electoral Roll → Official list of eligible voters
By-election → Election held to fill a vacant seat
Voter Turnout → Percentage of voters who cast their vote in an election
exam_tips: Remember that the minimum voting age in India is 18 years
The Election Commission of India ensures free and fair elections
Reserved constituencies ensure representation of SC and ST communities
Election symbols help voters identify political parties easily
High voter turnout indicates strong participation in democracy
